methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Late Stage → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.2x) to $400M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Defense & Security (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Defense & Security, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

data.narrative:
Destinus is a late-stage Defense & Security company that has raised over $400M to date. The broader sector comprises 10 comparable companies (2 public, 8 private), including peers such as Anduril Industries, Helsing, Shield AI, Saronic, and Quantum-Systems. A same-stage peer median approach was not applicable due to insufficient datapoints within the narrow Late Stage cohort in this sector.

The Stage-Progression method was the primary applicable triangulation approach. Using the $400M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a typical 1.2x step-up multiplier from Late Stage to the next funding stage yields an implied valuation of $480M. This reflects the conventional assumption that equity value materially exceeds cumulative capital deployed at this stage, though the magnitude of that premium remains constrained by limited comparable transaction data in the Defense & Security frontier-tech subset.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $408M (low) to $552M (high), with a median point of $480M. The range reflects reasonable upside and downside from the stage-adjusted midpoint. Key uncertainties include the absence of recent comparable transactions in this specific sector-stage intersection and the substantial capital intensity of defense-tech development, which may compress typical stage multiples. Any valuation conclusion should be stress-tested against revenue traction, customer concentration, and program-risk factors not present in the quantitative data.
